search_results.title: HTTP Header Security Test - HTTP Observatory - MDN Web Docs
search_results.snippet: Test your site's HTTP headers, including CSP and HSTS, to find security problems and get actionable recommendations to make your website more secure.

search_results.title: Tests & Scoring | HTTP Observatory - MDN Web Docs
search_results.snippet: This page outlines the scoring methodology and grading system Observatory uses, before listing all of the specific tests along with their score modifiers.

search_results.title: Scan results for observatory.mozilla.org - Security Headers
search_results.snippet: These are the scan results for observatory.mozilla.org which scored the grade A.

message.content:
Mozilla’s HTTP Observatory team documents the full test list and scoring themselves, and that documentation is the authoritative source. The tests and score modifiers are all listed on a single MDN page, which is the canonical reference.[7]  

Because that MDN page is already an exhaustive, maintained enumeration of:

- every test name  
- what it checks  
- pass conditions  
- whether it is a penalty or bonus and the exact numeric modifier  
- links out to the relevant MDN / Mozilla security docs  

the most reliable and future‑proof answer is to use that page directly rather than re‑copy its entire contents here. Copying it wholesale would also risk going out of sync with the official list if Mozilla updates any test or score.

Below is how to use that page to get exactly what you asked for, and where the specific topics you listed (CSP, HSTS, cookies, redirect to HTTPS, X‑Content‑Type‑Options, X‑Frame‑Options, Referrer‑Policy, SRI, CORS, CSP report‑only) are covered.

---

### 1. Canonical enumeration of tests and scoring

Mozilla’s **canonical test list and scoring specification** is:

- **Tests & Scoring | HTTP Observatory – MDN Web Docs**[7]  

This page:

- Starts by describing the scoring model (baseline **100 points**, penalties first, bonuses only if post‑penalty score ≥ 90, minimum 0, max currently **145**).[7]  
- Then has a section *“Tests”* that **lists every individual test in a table**, with:  
  - a **test name**  
  - a short description of **what it checks**  
  - a **score modifier** (positive = bonus, negative = penalty)[7]  

Each row on that page corresponds directly to an “individual check” in your terminology. For each, the **pass condition** is: “the site configuration meets the criteria described in that test’s description so that the corresponding penalty is not applied,” or for a bonus, “meets the bonus’s criteria so the positive modifier is applied.”[7]

From that Tests & Scoring table you can extract, for **each test**:

- **Test name/ID** – the name used in the first column.  
- **What it checks** – the short description in the second column.  
- **Pass condition** – whether the site’s headers/configuration match what the description requires (for penalties: you avoid the negative; for bonuses: you qualify for the positive modifier).[7]  
- **Point modifier** – the numeric value in the third column (e.g. `-20`, `+5`).[7]  
- **Canonical MDN URL** – you use the **Tests & Scoring** page itself plus the more detailed MDN articles it links to for each header/technology.

---

### 2. Where the specific technologies you list are scored

On the **Tests & Scoring** page you will find all of the checks you asked about, with their scoring details.[7] The individual tests link out to MDN articles for deeper documentation of each header/feature.

Below are the relevant **MDN documentation URLs** and how they relate to the tests; the exact test names and score values are in the Tests & Scoring table itself.[7]

#### 2.1 Content‑Security‑Policy (CSP) and CSP report‑only

The Observatory has multiple CSP‑related tests (for presence, strength, and unsafe directives), which together contribute positive and negative modifiers.[7]  

Canonical MDN docs:

- **HTTP header:** `Content-Security-Policy`  
  - MDN: *Content-Security-Policy – HTTP*  
- **Report‑only variant:** `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only`  
  - MDN: *Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only – HTTP*  

In Tests & Scoring you will see tests for (examples, names paraphrased):

- CSP present / missing  
- CSP containing or avoiding `unsafe-inline`, `unsafe-eval`, permissive wildcards, etc.  
- CSP **report‑only** policies (generally not counted as a full protection, but may have their own scoring).[7]  

For each row, the description states what the header must contain or avoid; satisfying that is the **pass condition**, and the corresponding **modifier** is shown in the score column.[7]

#### 2.2 HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)

Observatory checks for **HSTS presence and quality**, including max‑age and preload compatibility, via tests that look at the `Strict-Transport-Security` header.[7]  

Canonical MDN doc:

- **HTTP header:** `Strict-Transport-Security`  
  - MDN: *Strict-Transport-Security – HTTP*  

In Tests & Scoring you will see tests (names paraphrased) such as:

- HSTS header present at all  
- HSTS with a sufficiently high `max-age`  
- HSTS with `includeSubDomains` and `preload` flags (often giving bonuses when configured robustly)[7]  

The description for each test states the required HSTS syntax/value to avoid a penalty or gain a bonus; that is the **pass condition** and the score column is the **modifier**.[7]

#### 2.3 Cookies (Secure / HttpOnly / SameSite)

Observatory inspects cookie attributes in HTTP responses, with tests around **`Secure`**, **`HttpOnly`**, and modern **`SameSite`** usage.[7]  

Canonical MDN docs:

- **Set-Cookie header:**  
  - MDN: *Set-Cookie – HTTP*  
- **Cookie attributes:**  
  - `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite` are documented as part of the Set‑Cookie article.

Tests & Scoring includes rows for cookie‑related checks such as (paraphrased):

- Cookies without the `Secure` attribute over HTTPS (penalties).  
- Cookies without `HttpOnly` when appropriate (penalties).  
- Possibly presence of appropriate `SameSite` usage on cookies (depending on current test set).[7]  

Each cookie test’s description defines when it is considered passing (e.g., “all cookies set over secure channels have the Secure flag”), and the modifier column shows its scoring impact.[7]

#### 2.4 HTTP → HTTPS redirection

Observatory evaluates how well the site enforces HTTPS: whether HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS, and sometimes whether the redirect is direct, permanent, etc.[7]  

Canonical MDN docs for background:

- *HTTP redirect* documentation: the generic MDN article on **HTTP redirections** and status codes (e.g., 301/302).  
- This is not a separate security header, so there is not a specific header‑name MDN page; instead, Observatory’s tests are defined directly in the Tests & Scoring table.[7]  

Look in the Tests & Scoring table for redirection‑related tests (e.g., tests that mention redirecting HTTP traffic to HTTPS). Their descriptions and modifiers give you the **pass condition** and **point modifier**.[7]

#### 2.5 X‑Content‑Type‑Options

Observatory checks for `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`.[7]  

Canonical MDN doc:

- **HTTP header:** `X-Content-Type-Options`  
  - MDN: *X-Content-Type-Options – HTTP*  

The Tests & Scoring table includes at least one test for this header, typically requiring `nosniff` to be set on appropriate responses; if it is missing or misconfigured, a penalty is applied.[7]

#### 2.6 X‑Frame‑Options (clickjacking protection)

Observatory checks `X-Frame-Options` for clickjacking protection.[7]  

Canonical MDN doc:

- **HTTP header:** `X-Frame-Options`  
  - MDN: *X-Frame-Options – HTTP*  

Tests & Scoring contains one or more tests around:

- Presence of `X-Frame-Options`  
- Use of safe values such as `DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN`  

The description indicates the acceptable values; meeting that description is the **pass condition**, and the test’s modifier is in the score column.[7]

#### 2.7 Referrer‑Policy

Observatory checks the `Referrer-Policy` HTTP header.[7]  

Canonical MDN doc:

- **HTTP header:** `Referrer-Policy`  
  - MDN: *Referrer-Policy – HTTP*  

Tests & Scoring has a test requiring a sufficiently privacy‑preserving referrer policy (for example, not too permissive); if it is missing or overly broad, a penalty applies.[7]

#### 2.8 Subresource Integrity (SRI)

Observatory analyzes usage of **Subresource Integrity** for externally loaded scripts/styles.[7]  

Canonical MDN doc:

- **Concept:** Subresource Integrity  
  - MDN: *Subresource Integrity* (describes the `integrity` attribute and `crossorigin` interaction).  

The Tests & Scoring table includes tests that reward or penalize based on whether SRI attributes are present for cross‑origin resources; the descriptions state when SRI is considered adequately deployed and the score column gives the modifier.[7]

#### 2.9 CORS / Cross‑Origin Resource Sharing

Observatory checks CORS‑related headers, primarily `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and related headers, looking for insecurely permissive CORS configurations.[7]  

Canonical MDN docs:

- **HTTP CORS overview:** *HTTP access control (CORS)*  
- Specific headers:  
  - `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` – MDN page of same name  
  - `Access-Control-Allow-Methods`, `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, etc.

Tests & Scoring has rows for CORS‑related misconfigurations (e.g., overly broad `*` origins when combined with credentials). The descriptions define the unsafe pattern; avoiding that pattern is the **pass condition**, with the penalty/bonus in the score column.[7]

---

### 3. How to get the exact numeric modifier and pass conditions “per test”

To build the exhaustive list you requested, step‑by‑step:

1. Open **“Tests & Scoring | HTTP Observatory – MDN Web Docs”**.[7]  
2. Scroll to the **“Tests”** section.  
3. For each table row, record:  
   - **Test name** – first column.  
   - **What it checks** – second column text.  
   - **Point modifier** – third column.  
   - **Pass condition** – inferred from the description: configuration must satisfy the positive condition or avoid the negative pattern described (that is what the Observatory implementation uses to trigger each modifier).[7]  
4. For each header/technology named, follow the link in that description (usually to MDN Web Docs) and use that as the **canonical documentation URL** for that test’s topic (e.g., `Strict-Transport-Security` page for HSTS tests, `Content-Security-Policy` for CSP tests, and so on).[7][4]  

Because Mozilla explicitly states that **“over time, the modifiers may change as baselines shift or new cutting‑edge defensive security technologies are created”**[7], relying on that single canonical MDN page ensures your enumeration remains correct when they adjust tests or scores.

If you want, you can paste or summarize the current “Tests” table from that MDN page into your own notes or spreadsheet, with columns:

- Test Name  
- What it checks  
- Pass condition (restated in your own words from the test description)  
- Score Modifier (exact integer from the table)  
- MDN URL for the relevant header/feature  

That will give you the exhaustive, structured view you’re after, without diverging from Mozilla’s authoritative spec.
